back: A set is countable if it is finite or if its elements can be put into a one-to-one correspondence with the set of natural numbers (i.e., it is countably infinite).
A set is uncountable if it is infinite but cannot be put into a one-to-one correspondence with the set of natural numbers. Its cardinality is strictly greater than that of the natural numbers.

front: What is the cardinality of the set of real numbers?
back: The set of real numbers R is uncountable. Its cardinality is denoted by c (continuum) and is strictly greater than ℵ₀ (aleph-null), the cardinality of the natural numbers.

front: State one key technique used to prove a set is uncountable.
back: Cantor's diagonal argument is a common technique used to prove that a set, such as the real numbers between 0 and 1, is uncountable by showing that any attempted listing of its elements is necessarily incomplete.

front: Is the union of countably many countable sets itself countable?
back: Yes, the union of a countable collection of countable sets is also countable. This is a fundamental result in set theory.
